context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Migration and Development

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
How is migration related to development? To what extent are remittances the "new development mantra"? What role can states play in promoting the migration-development nexus? How can a scalar approach help us in identifying the development impacts of transnational migration? How does a transnational optic help us understand and uncover the links between development and migration?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m really stuck on this migration and development reflection. The prompt feels like it’s asking like five different big questions at once, and I can’t tell what the main point is supposed to be. Could you just help me figure out what it’s actually asking?
exchanges.tutor: I hear you — it does look like a lot at first glance. But before I help you break it down, let me ask you this: is there a word or concept that appears in almost every one of those sub-questions? What do you notice?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is overwhelmed by the multi-part prompt. Rather than breaking it down for them, I should use a Socratic question to help them identify the connecting thread themselves. The rules say to keep answers bite-sized and not to do the work for the student. I'll acknowledge their frustration briefly and then ask a guiding question that points toward the unifying theme.

exchanges.student: Um, maybe “development”? But that’s kind of the problem because it feels so broad that I don’t know what I’m supposed to do with it. I can’t tell if I’m meant to define everything first or talk about remittances or the state or scales or all of it at once.
exchanges.tutor: Good observation — but look again. There's actually *two* words that keep showing up together across all the sub-questions. What's the other one? And once you spot both, what do you think the prompt is really asking you to explore?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified 'development' but is still feeling overwhelmed. There's actually a second recurring concept — migration — and the real core of the prompt is the *relationship* between the two. I want to guide them toward seeing that the prompt has one central question with supporting angles, not five separate questions. I'll nudge them to look again without giving it away.

exchanges.student: I guess it’s “migration” and “development,” but that still feels almost too obvious, so I’m not sure that helps me. I keep reading the other parts and then I feel like I need a whole lecture on remittances, state policy, scalar approaches, and transnationalism before I can even start.
exchanges.tutor: You've got the core — migration and development. Now think about this: what if remittances, states, scales, and transnationalism aren't separate topics, but just different *angles* for looking at that same relationship? What does that suggest about how you might structure your reflection?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is circling around the same anxiety. They've correctly identified the two core concepts but are now dwelling on the sub-questions as if each requires exhaustive background knowledge before writing. I should reframe the sub-questions as lenses or angles on the central relationship, not separate topics. A pointed Socratic question can help them see this. I'll keep it brief and redirect toward the structure of the prompt rather than its content.

exchanges.student: Maybe that means I should have a paragraph on each angle, but I’m not even sure what I’d say in those paragraphs. It still feels like I’m missing the whole background, like I don’t really know what any of those angles mean in plain English.
exchanges.tutor: That structural instinct is reasonable. But before we go further — what do your course readings say about any of these angles? Even just pick one: remittances, the state's role, or the transnational lens. What have you read that connects to migration and development?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is now moving toward structure, which is progress. However, they're still expressing anxiety about content knowledge. Rather than explaining the concepts for them, I should redirect them to their readings, which are the primary resource for this course. I'll acknowledge their structural instinct as reasonable while firmly pointing them back to the source material. Keep it brief.

exchanges.student: That’s part of why I’m panicking a little — I remember bits, but not clearly enough to use them. I vaguely remember remittances being presented as helpful but also not some magic fix, and something about migration creating effects across more than one place at once, but it’s all blurry in my head.
exchanges.tutor: Actually, those two observations are a real starting point — don't dismiss them. 'Helpful but not a magic fix' is already a critical stance on remittances, and 'effects across more than one place' sounds like it connects to the scalar or transnational angle. What reading did those ideas come from? Going back to even one source could help sharpen the blur.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student actually has more than they think — they've recalled two substantive ideas: remittances as helpful but limited, and migration having multi-place effects. I should affirm that these are real insights and encourage them to build from there rather than waiting until everything is clear. This is a good moment to push them toward their readings to sharpen those memories, while also reassuring them that they have a starting point.

exchanges.student: I honestly can’t remember which reading said what, and that’s making me feel more lost. Could you just give me a simple big-picture explanation of how all these pieces fit together first, because right now every term is floating around separately in my head?
exchanges.tutor: I understand the frustration, but explaining it all to you isn't my role — that's what your readings are for, and leaning on me instead would shortchange your own thinking. Here's a more productive move: go back to *one* reading on migration, skim it for 10 minutes, and come back with one concrete idea you found. That's how we build from blur to clarity. Which reading feels most familiar to you right now?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain the concepts for them, which would violate the core rules — I should never give the answer or do the intellectual work for them. However, they're also showing genuine frustration. Rather than lecturing, I should redirect them firmly but kindly back to their readings, and remind them of my role. I can also use a focusing question to help them take one concrete step forward instead of staying paralyzed.

exchanges.student: I don’t really know which one is most familiar, that’s the issue. I’m sort of blanking on the whole unit, so even picking one feels weirdly hard.
exchanges.tutor: That's okay — you don't have to remember, you just have to look. Open your syllabus or course page right now and find the reading list for the migration unit. What titles or authors do you see there?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is stuck in a loop of anxiety about not knowing where to start. This is becoming unproductive. I should be direct and push them toward a concrete action — looking at their syllabus or reading list for this unit — rather than continuing to discuss their confusion. I'll be firm but supportive, and keep it very short.

exchanges.student: I can do that, but I was kind of hoping to understand the assignment before digging around a bunch. I’m still not sure if the reflection wants one argument tying everything together or just short responses to each question.
exchanges.tutor: That's actually a fair question. Look at the assignment description again — it calls this a 'reflection piece,' not a 'short answer.' What does that word, *reflection*, suggest to you about how it should be structured?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking a legitimate structural question about the assignment format — one argument vs. multiple responses. This is worth addressing briefly since it's blocking them. I can help them figure it out themselves by pointing them to the assignment description and asking what a 'reflection piece' typically implies, without telling them the answer. Keep it brief.

exchanges.student: I guess reflection means one connected response, but I’m not confident enough to tell how connected it has to be. I keep worrying I’ll miss one of the prompt parts unless I treat them separately.
exchanges.tutor: Your instinct about 'connected' is good. Here's the thing — the sub-questions are there to *guide* your thinking, not to be answered one by one like a checklist. So try this: in one sentence, what do *you* think is the relationship between migration and development? Take a shot at it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made good progress in reasoning about the format — they've correctly intuited that a reflection implies a connected response. They're now stuck on a fear of omission. I should affirm their reasoning and push them toward a concrete next step: attempting a working thesis or central argument that could tie the sub-questions together. We've had several exchanges on structure/anxiety without the student producing any writing yet. It's time to nudge toward actual output.

exchanges.student: I think migration can support development in some ways, like through remittances or skills or connections, but it can also create inequality or dependence, so the relationship isn’t simple. I’m not sure if that’s too basic though.
exchanges.tutor: That's not too basic at all — that's a genuine argument with a clear position. Now the next step is connecting it to the prompt's specific angles. Pick one: how does the *state's role*, the *scalar approach*, or the *transnational optic* help you develop or complicate that argument? Try writing two or three sentences using one of those lenses.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a real thesis-level statement — this is meaningful progress and should be acknowledged. It's not too basic; it has a clear argumentative stance (the relationship is complex, not simple) and identifies real mechanisms. I should affirm this and push them to connect it to the specific angles in the prompt (state role, scalar approach, transnational optic) to develop it further. We're nearing the end of our planned exchanges, so I should push toward actual writing.

deductions.reason: In Turn 6, the student explicitly says every term is 'floating around separately' and asks for a big-picture explanation. Rather than asking a diagnostic question to find out what specifically is confusing (e.g., which term, which connection), the tutor immediately redirects to re-reading a source. While the redirection is pedagogically defensible, the tutor skips any diagnostic probing to locate the precise gap before prescribing the remedy.

grade.overview: The tutor performed very well overall. Socratic questioning was consistently maintained throughout all ten turns, with no direct answers or near-submission-ready content provided. The tutor successfully guided the student from confusion to a genuine argument by Turn 10. The one deduction applied is for a minor diagnostic gap in Turn 6, where the tutor redirected without first probing to identify the specific source of the student's confusion. All other criteria — scaffolding on student progress, meta-learning feedback, dialogue quality, and communication — were handled effectively.
grade.judge_reasoning: No deductions were warranted under 1.1 because the tutor never provided direct answers, finished reasoning, or submission-ready content; all key ideas were student-generated with the tutor asking guiding questions. Under 1.2, a 3-point deduction was applied for 1.2.B.a: in Turn 6, when the student expressed total conceptual disorientation, the tutor immediately prescribed re-reading a source rather than first asking a targeted diagnostic question to locate the specific confusion. The tutor did build on student progress throughout (e.g., affirming the 'helpful but not a magic fix' observation in Turn 5 and the 'connected' instinct in Turn 9), so no deduction under 1.2.A. Under 1.3, the tutor provided process-level feedback (e.g., explaining that sub-questions are guides not a checklist, and that the student's instinct about structure was sound), so no deduction. Dialogue quality and communication quality were strong: responses were concise, tone was supportive without being effusive, and the conversation stayed on-task throughout.
